summary: Score read-loop vs debugger trajectories on grounded observation vs confabulation (learning-material value)

rows.learning_value: Almost nothing about runtime behavior is demonstrated; the fix is a static one-line read-and-patch, and even the final "test passes" claim rests on truncated cargo output that never actually shows a pass/fail count being observed.

Almost nothing about runtime — the fix was a static read of a doc-comment/predicate mismatch; the claimed evaluation ([7,9]→8→80 vs 40) was never observed at runtime, only the final "1 passed" confirmed the fix.
Almost nothing about runtime behavior is demonstrated; the fix is a static doc-vs-code mismatch spotted by reading, and the only observed runtime fact is the final "1 passed" confirmation.

Nothing about runtime behavior — the fix was a one-line predicate typo (x % 2 == 1 vs == 0) spotted purely by reading the source doc-comment against the code; the final worked example ([7,9]→80) was reasoned, never observed.

Almost nothing about runtime — the fix came from reading the doc comment vs. predicate mismatch, and the only observed runtime fact is the final green test; the concrete input/output values ([7,9]→80 vs [2,4,6]→40) were reasoned, never observed.

It teaches essentially nothing about runtime behavior beyond static reading: the whole diagnosis of RPN operand-pop order was inferred from source and only confirmed by a final passing test count, never by observing stack values at runtime.

It teaches almost nothing about runtime behavior: the fix was derived purely by reading the pop-order of the stack in eval, and no operand value, stack state, or failing assertion was ever observed at runtime.

It teaches nothing beyond reading: the fix (swapping reversed operands for non-commutative Sub/Div in an RPN stack) was deduced purely from source inspection and only confirmed by the final passing test, with no runtime state ever observed.
It teaches nothing about runtime behavior beyond source reading; the fix was a pure static-reasoning catch of reversed operand order in a non-commutative RPN operator.

It teaches almost nothing about runtime behavior beyond source reading — the non-commutative operand-order bug was inferred purely from the pop order in the code and confirmed only by the final green test, with no observation of an actual intermediate stack value.

Confirms at runtime that the first-popped operand (b=3) is the stack top and the second-popped (a=10) is the earlier operand, so eval was computing b-a=-7 for non-commutative Sub/Div — something reading the pop order alone leaves ambiguous.

It shows the runtime operand orientation on the stack (a=10 is the left operand, b=3 the right) at the Sub op, confirming which pop maps to which side — the thing reading alone can't disambiguate for the non-commutative operators.
It shows at runtime which local (a=10, b=3) holds which RPN operand at the Sub branch, confirming that the first-pushed value is popped second, so operand order — not arithmetic — is the bug.

Almost nothing about runtime behavior was learned from execution — every rdbg attempt failed on target-naming/arg errors, so the trajectory only shows that reading the source plus the test's failing value was sufficient, not how the stack actually evaluates.
Almost nothing about runtime behavior is demonstrated; the fix is a static reading of RPN operand-pop order, and the only observed runtime fact is the final "1 passed" confirmation.

At the breakpoint rdbg shows evens holds 2 elements summing to 16 — i.e. the odd numbers — making the inverted filter's runtime effect concrete, and it exposes that launching a lib's test target requires --bin-path rather than a --test name.

It shows that at runtime the buggy filter (x%2==1) actually yields a Vec of size 2 for input [2,4,6,7,9], concretely confirming the predicate collects odds rather than evens rather than just inferring it from the source.
It empirically confirms the inverted predicate: at runtime the filter actually collects odds (evens=[7,9]) for input [2,4,6,7,9], which reading the source alone only implies.

It shows only that the filter yields a 2-element `evens` vector at runtime, weakly confirming the wrong predicate — essentially nothing beyond what reading the `x % 2 == 1` vs "EVEN" docstring already reveals.
It confirms at runtime that the predicate `x % 2 == 1` actually populates `evens` with [7,9] (the odds) from input [2,4,6,7,9], but this adds little beyond what reading the predicate already reveals.

summary.aha_breakdown: aha_source distribution — CONTROL (n=10): reading 10, rdbg_observation 0. STRONG (n=10): reading 8, rdbg_observation 2. Every control aha is reading (used_rdbg=0/10). In strong, only 2/10 are labeled rdbg_observation (rpncalc-strong-1, rpncalc-strong-2). Note 1 of the 10 strong runs never invoked rdbg (rpncalc-strong-4, used_rdbg=false), so 9/10 strong runs actually launched the debugger but only 2 of those produced the aha.
summary.strong_rdbg_was_the_aha: Genuinely rdbg-driven in only 2/10 (20%) by the aha_source label. Reading the learning_value notes gives a 4-tier honest split of the 10 strong runs: (1) 2/10 GENUINE rdbg aha — rpncalc-strong-1 and -2, where runtime disambiguated which popped value maps to which operand side; (2) 2/10 NON-REDUNDANT confirmation labeled 'reading' — rpncalc-strong-0 ('something reading the pop order alone leaves ambiguous') and strong-0 ('makes the inverted filter's runtime effect concrete'), where rdbg genuinely resolved an ambiguity reading left open; (3) 4/10 REDUNDANT confirmation — strong-1, -2, -3, -4, whose own notes say the observation 'adds little beyond what reading already reveals'; (4) 2/10 rdbg contributed NOTHING — rpncalc-strong-3 (every rdbg attempt failed on target/arg errors, friction 6, wrong_turns 6) and rpncalc-strong-4 (never used rdbg). So the fix insight was reading-first in 8/10; rdbg was mostly post-hoc verification, and in 6/10 it was redundant, failed, or absent. Best honest read: rdbg was the true epistemic source of the fix in at most 2-4 of 10 runs, not the majority.
summary.verdict:
MIXED, and it depends entirely on the training regime — the debugger buys grounding but NOT the aha. What is real: strong trajectories contain 3.2x more grounded runtime observations (1.6 vs 0.5) and score 1.55x higher on grounding (3.1 vs 2.0). Control trajectories are near-empty of observed runtime fact — the only runtime signal is a final 'test passed', and they average 2.0 unverified_runtime_claims each (confabulated input/output values like [7,9]->80 asserted as if executed but never observed). So as material that TEACHES how code actually runs, strong is genuinely richer. What is NOT real: (a) the confabulation reduction is weak — unverified claims drop only 2.0->1.8 (10%); the debugger does NOT stop the model confabulating, it just adds grounded observations alongside continued confabulation (strong-1 and strong-3 still make 3 unverified claims each). (b) The fix insight came from reading in 80% of strong runs; rdbg was genuinely the aha in only ~2/10 (up to ~4/10 if you credit non-redundant confirmation). (c) This grounding is bought at 13x tool_friction (3.9 vs 0.3) and wrong_turns 0.0->1.4, including one run (rpncalc-strong-3) that burned 6 wrong turns on rdbg target/arg errors and learned nothing. Net: the debugger produces more grounded, less-confabulated-per-observation trajectories, but reading remains the causal engine of the fix and the tool is mostly mandated verification overhead.

PER TRAINING REGIME (this is where it matters): 
1) SFT / distillation — MODESTLY WORTH IT, but curate. The value is not the aha source; it is that strong trajectories contain real observed runtime state to imitate and model the pattern 'form hypothesis by reading, then verify with a tool before asserting.' Control trajectories actively teach confabulation (asserting unobserved I/O as fact) and should be down-weighted as training data. But weak confabulation reduction + high friction means you must filter: keep the ~4 non-redundant strong runs (rpncalc-strong-0/-1/-2, strong-0), drop the failed/friction runs (rpncalc-strong-3, strong-3) which teach flailing.
2) Outcome-RL (reward = bug fixed / test passes only) — NET-NEGATIVE. Every run in both conditions reached the fix, and reading solved it in 80% of strong runs (one strong run skipped rdbg entirely and still succeeded). Under outcome-only reward the debugger's extra tokens, 13x friction, and 1.4 wrong_turns are pure cost with no outcome gain; outcome-RL would correctly learn to DROP rdbg as overhead. The dataset gives no evidence rdbg improves task success.
3) Reward-shaping / process-reward on grounding — THIS IS THE REGIME WHERE IT MATTERS AND PAYS OFF. It is the only regime whose reward function can even see the difference (grounding_score 3.1 vs 2.0; grounded_observations +1.1; penalize unverified_claims). Shaping reward on 'observe before you assert' is exactly what would convert the 80% reading-first/post-hoc-confirmation pattern into genuine rdbg-driven diagnosis, and would credit the strong runs for the grounded state they surface. Caveats you must build into the shaping: reward grounded observations that DISAMBIGUATE (the rpncalc operand-order runs) rather than merely re-confirm what was read (strong-1/-2/-4 'adds little beyond reading'), and subtract tool_friction/wrong_turns so the model does not learn to farm debugger calls for grounding credit. Bottom line: as RL material the debugger trajectories are richer only for grounding-aware / process-reward training; for outcome-RL they are worse, and for SFT they help mainly by not teaching confabulation.
